agent_output:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five significant architectural anti-patterns accumulated over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), the synchronous notification fan-in (creating a availability bottleneck), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). Left unaddressed, these patterns will produce cascading failures, make independent deployment impossible, and increasingly slow feature delivery. The remediation plan is sequenced to address highest-risk items first while minimizing disruption.",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "Order Service", "responsibility": "Order lifecycle management", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Courier, database read directly by Reporting" },
      { "name": "Courier Service", "responsibility": "Delivery assignment and tracking", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service, no independent data ownership" },
      { "name": "Notification Service", "responsibility": "Send notifications across channels", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — synchronously coupled to 5 upstream callers, single point of failure" },
      { "name": "Customer Service", "responsibility": "Customer identity, preferences, addresses", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes payment methods and order history that belong to other domains" },
      { "name": "Payment Service", "responsibility": "Payment processing and refunds", "boundaries": "PARTIALLY VIOLATED — refund processing exposed as REST endpoint called by cron" },
      { "name": "Reporting Service", "responsibility": "Business analytics and reporting", "boundaries": "VIOLATED — directly reads Order Service database tables, bypassing API contract" }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + platform" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + platform" },
      { "from": "Payment Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + platform" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + platform" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Notification Service", "contract": "Synchronous HTTP call", "coupling_type": "temporal + platform" },
      { "from": "Order Service", "to": "Shared PostgreSQL", "contract": "Direct table access (order_ schema prefix)", "coupling_type": "database-level (strongest possible)" },
      { "from": "Courier Service", "to": "Shared PostgreSQL", "contract": "Direct table access (courier_ schema prefix)", "coupling_type": "database-level (strongest possible)" },
      { "from": "Reporting Service", "to": "Order Service DB", "contract": "Direct SQL reads against order tables", "coupling_type": "database-level (strongest possible)" },
      { "from": "Cron Job", "to": "Payment Service", "contract": "HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/process-refunds", "coupling_type": "temporal + protocol misuse" },
      { "from": "Customer Service", "to": "Multiple domains", "contract": "GET /api/v1/everything — monolithic response", "coupling_type": "semantic (god object)" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Currently: Services share data via direct database access and synchronous HTTP calls. No event-driven communication. Reporting bypasses service APIs entirely. Background work masquerades as HTTP requests. Target: Each service owns its data exclusively, communicates via async events for notifications and cross-service data needs, and background jobs run as internal processes.",
    "patterns_used": [
      { "pattern": "Shared Database (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Likely started as expedient shortcut when Order and Courier were part of one service" },
      { "pattern": "Synchronous Fan-In (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Notification started as simple utility; each new service added another synchronous caller" },
      { "pattern": "God Endpoint (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Customer Service aggregates data from multiple domains into one response, likely driven by a frontend that needed everything at once" },
      { "pattern": "HTTP-as-Job-Trigger (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Developers reused the existing REST framework to expose job functionality rather than implementing proper job infrastructure" },
      { "pattern": "Direct Database Read across service boundary (ANTI-PATTERN)", "rationale": "Reporting needed query flexibility that the Order API didn't provide, so it went straight to the tables" }
    ]
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLineItem", "OrderStatus"] },
      { "name": "Courier & Delivery", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Delivery", "CourierAssignment", "Route"] },
      { "name": "Customer Identity", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "Address", "Preferences"] },
      { "name": "Payment", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Payment", "Refund", "PaymentMethod"] },
      { "name": "Notification", "classification": "generic", "aggregates": ["NotificationRequest", "NotificationTemplate", "DeliveryChannel"] },
      { "name": "Reporting & Analytics", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Report", "ReportDefinition"] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Courier & Delivery", "relationship": "CURRENTLY: Shared Kernel via shared DB. TARGET: Customer-Supplier with domain events" },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Reporting & Analytics", "relationship": "CURRENTLY: Direct DB read (Conformist to schema). TARGET: Published Language via read-model/CDC" },
      { "upstream": "All Services", "downstream": "Notification", "relationship": "CURRENTLY: Synchronous RPC. TARGET: Open Host Service consuming domain events" },
      { "upstream": "Payment", "downstream": "Customer Identity", "relationship": "CURRENTLY: Payment methods leaked into Customer god endpoint. TARGET: Separate contexts with ACL" }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Introduce message broker for async notification delivery",
      "gains": "Eliminates temporal coupling for 5 services, Notification outage no longer cascades, enables retry/DLQ semantics",
      "sacrifices": "Operational complexity of running a broker, eventual consistency for notification delivery confirmation, new failure mode (message loss)",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Async HTTP with webhooks — simpler but doesn't solve fan-in", "Outbox pattern without broker — still requires polling infrastructure"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Split shared database into two independent databases",
      "gains": "Independent deployment, independent scaling, independent schema evolution, true service autonomy",
      "sacrifices": "Cross-service queries become harder, requires data migration with downtime or dual-write period, temporary complexity during transition",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Schema-level isolation with strict access controls — insufficient, still couples deployment", "Database views as API — still shared infrastructure"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace direct DB reads for Reporting with CDC or read-model",
      "gains": "Order Service can evolve schema freely, Reporting gets optimized read model, clear ownership boundary",
      "sacrifices": "Data freshness (seconds of lag), CDC infrastructure cost, more moving parts",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Add API endpoints to Order Service for Reporting — may not support complex query patterns", "Materialized views in separate schema — still database-coupled"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Decompose /api/v1/everything into domain-specific endpoints",
      "gains": "Each service owns its data contract, independent caching, smaller payloads, follows Interface Segregation Principle",
      "sacrifices": "Frontend must make multiple calls (or use BFF/GraphQL), migration burden on existing consumers",
      "alternatives_considered": ["GraphQL gateway — good long-term but high upfront investment", "BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) — pragmatic middle ground"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Replace HTTP-triggered jobs with proper job runner",
      "gains": "Job lifecycle management, retry semantics, concurrency control, no accidental external triggering, visibility into job status",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure component to operate, migration effort for existing jobs",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Keep HTTP but add auth + idempotency — lipstick on a pig", "Kubernetes CronJobs calling internal methods — better but K8s-dependent"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Notification delivery mechanism", "evolution_path": "Once event-driven, can easily add new notification channels (SMS, push, in-app) without touching upstream services" },
    { "area": "Reporting data sources", "evolution_path": "CDC/read-model approach allows adding new data sources without coupling to upstream schemas" },
    { "area": "Customer API surface", "evolution_path": "Decomposed endpoints allow independent versioning and evolution of each domain's customer-facing data" },
    { "area": "Background job infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Proper job framework allows adding scheduled jobs, priority queues, and workflow orchestration" }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "Message broker choice", "reason": "Once chosen (RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS), migration is expensive. Choose deliberately." },
    { "area": "Event schema format", "reason": "Domain event contracts become the integration backbone. Must be versioned from day one with a schema registry or at minimum a documented contract." },
    { "area": "Database-per-service boundary", "reason": "Once split, re-merging databases is extremely painful. The split must follow correct domain boundaries." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Shared database allows Courier Service schema changes to break Order Service (and vice versa) with zero warning", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Priority 1: Split into separate databases. Interim: add integration tests that validate cross-schema assumptions" },
    { "risk": "Notification Service outage cascades to all 5 upstream services via synchronous calls, potentially causing system-wide failure", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Priority 2: Move to async event-driven notifications. Interim: add circuit breakers and timeouts on all synchronous notification calls immediately" },
    { "risk": "Reporting Service's direct DB reads create invisible coupling — Order schema migration silently breaks reports", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Priority 3: Introduce CDC or replicated read-model. Interim: add Order schema change checklist that includes Reporting impact review" },
    { "risk": "/api/v1/everything endpoint leaks payment methods (PCI scope) to any consumer of the Customer Service", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Priority 4: Decompose endpoint. Payment methods must be served only by Payment Service with proper access controls" },
    { "risk": "HTTP-based job endpoints are externally accessible — /api/v1/jobs/process-refunds could be triggered by unauthorized callers", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Immediate: add authentication/IP restriction. Priority 5: migrate to internal job runner" },
    { "risk": "Data migration during database split may cause data loss or inconsistency if not carefully orchestrated", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Use expand-contract migration: dual-write phase, verify consistency, then cut over" },
    { "risk": "Introducing async messaging adds new failure modes (message loss, ordering issues, poison messages)", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Use transactional outbox pattern, implement DLQ with alerting, design consumers to be idempotent" }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Execute in 5 phases, sequenced by risk severity and dependency. Each phase should be independently deployable and provide value even if subsequent phases are delayed.\n\nPHASE 0 — IMMEDIATE (Week 1-2): Apply band-aids to critical risks.\n  • Add circuit breakers + 2s timeouts on all sync Notification calls\n  • Add authentication/IP-whitelist to job endpoints\n  • Add cross-schema integration tests for Order/Courier shared DB\n\nPHASE 1 — SPLIT SHARED DATABASE (Weeks 3-8):\n  • Identify all cross-schema queries between Order and Courier\n  • Define API contracts for data that Courier needs from Order (and vice versa)\n  • Implement APIs on both services\n  • Dual-write migration: both services write to old DB and new DB\n  • Verify consistency, then cut over Courier to its own PostgreSQL instance\n\nPHASE 2 — ASYNC NOTIFICATIONS (Weeks 5-10, can overlap with Phase 1):\n  • Deploy message broker (recommend RabbitMQ for this scale; Kafka if you anticipate event sourcing needs)\n  • Define domain events: OrderPlaced, DeliveryAssigned, PaymentProcessed, etc.\n  • Notification Service subscribes to relevant events\n  • Migrate one upstream service at a time (start with Reporting — lowest risk)\n  • Remove synchronous calls only after async path is verified\n\nPHASE 3 — REPORTING DECOUPLING (Weeks 9-14):\n  • Implement CDC from Order DB to a Reporting read-model (Debezium + dedicated reporting DB)\n  • Migrate Reporting queries to read-model\n  • Remove direct DB access grants\n\nPHASE 4 — CUSTOMER API DECOMPOSITION (Weeks 12-18):\n  • Identify all consumers of /api/v1/everything\n  • Create focused endpoints: /api/v1/customers/{id}/profile, /addresses, /preferences\n  • Payment methods served only via Payment Service\n  • Implement BFF if frontend needs aggregated data\n  • Deprecate /api/v1/everything with sunset header\n\nPHASE 5 — JOB INFRASTRUCTURE (Weeks 14-18):\n  • Adopt a job framework (Sidekiq, Hangfire, Bull, or cloud-native equivalent)\n  • Migrate process-refunds and other jobs to internal job definitions\n  • Remove HTTP job endpoints\n  • Add job dashboard for visibility",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "docs/adr/001-split-order-courier-database.md",
      "docs/adr/002-async-notification-events.md",
      "docs/adr/003-reporting-read-model.md",
      "docs/adr/004-customer-api-decomposition.md",
      "docs/adr/005-background-job-infrastructure.md",
      "docs/architecture/domain-events-catalog.md",
      "docs/architecture/context-map.md",
      "order-service/src/api/courier-integration-api.ts (new API for Courier to consume)",
      "notification-service/src/consumers/domain-event-consumer.ts",
      "shared/events/schemas/ (domain event schemas with versioning)"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig for all migrations (run old and new in parallel, shift traffic gradually). Transactional Outbox for reliable event publishing. Anti-Corruption Layer between contexts during transition. Expand-Contract for database migrations. Circuit Breaker on all synchronous cross-service calls.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "Do NOT attempt a big-bang migration of the shared database — use dual-write with consistency verification",
      "Do NOT introduce events without idempotent consumers — you will get duplicate processing",
      "Do NOT decompose the Customer API without first identifying ALL consumers — breaking unknown callers is worse than the current god endpoint",
      "Do NOT choose Kafka just because it is popular — RabbitMQ is simpler to operate and sufficient unless you need event replay or log-style retention",
      "Do NOT remove synchronous notification calls before the async path is proven in production under load",
      "Do NOT split the database along technical lines (tables vs schemas) — split along domain boundaries (Order vs Courier)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 82,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Anti-pattern identification — these are well-documented, widely recognized architectural smells",
      "Remediation sequencing — risk-severity ordering is clear from the described symptoms",
      "Shared database split — this is a well-understood migration pattern with established playbooks",
      "Async notification migration — standard event-driven architecture transition"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact scope of cross-schema coupling between Order and Courier — would need to scan actual queries to assess migration difficulty",
      "Number and nature of /api/v1/everything consumers — unknown consumer count affects decomposition risk",
      "Current traffic volumes and latency budgets — affects broker choice and CDC strategy",
      "Existing monitoring and observability maturity — affects ability to safely execute strangler fig migrations"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Services are independently deployable (separate processes/containers) despite the shared database",
      "The team has capacity to operate a message broker",
      "No hard real-time requirements on notification delivery (seconds of delay is acceptable)",
      "The organization can tolerate a 4-5 month remediation timeline with incremental delivery",
      "PostgreSQL is the only shared persistence — no shared Redis, Elasticsearch, or other data stores creating additional coupling"
    ]
  }
}
agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five significant architectural anti-patterns accumulated over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), the synchronous notification fan-in (creating a availability bottleneck), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling). Left unaddressed, these patterns will produce cascading failures, make independent deployment
